p14.page_title: IV. EXPLANATION OF THE USE OF COLORS IN THE SCHEMES AND TEXT

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A protected glycal undergoes a [2+2] cycloaddition with dichloroketene, generated in situ from trichloroacetyl chloride and a zinc-copper couple, to form a bicyclic dichlorocyclobutanone adduct.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: In most (but not all) schemes the starting molecule is colored blue, while the reagent or the reaction partner may be of any of the remaining two colors (red and green). The newly formed bonds are always black.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A bicyclic amino alcohol with a cyano group is treated with potassium hydride and 18-crown-6 in THF, forming a potassium alkoxide intermediate with a diazo group. This intermediate undergoes a [3,3] 2-aza-Cope rearrangement to yield a bicyclic ketone product as its potassium enolate.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: In the case of complex rearrangements, numbering of the initial carbon skeleton has been applied in addition to the colors to facilitate understanding. Again, the newly formed bonds are black.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A multi-step synthesis towards Epothilone B is shown. An enoate with an oxazolidinone chiral auxiliary is treated sequentially with NaHMDS, a diphenylsulfinyl-substituted epoxide, and CSA to yield a hydroxy acid intermediate with 71% yield over three steps. This intermediate is then converted to Epothilone B in further steps.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: In most instances, the product of a given named reaction/process will be part of a larger structure (e.g., natural product) at the end of the described synthetic effort. For pedagogical reasons, the authors decided to indicate where the building block appears in the target structure. It is the authors' hope that the reader will be able to put the named reaction/process in context and the provided synthetic example will not be just an abstract one.

extracts.transformation_text: Synthesis of Epothilone B precursor
extracts.reactants_text: enoate with oxazolidinone auxiliary and THP ether
extracts.notes_text: The product is an intermediate in the synthesis of Epothilone B.

p53.page_title: VII. NAMED ORGANIC REACTIONS IN ALPHABETICAL ORDER
page_section_hints: NAMED ORGANIC REACTIONS IN ALPHABETICAL ORDER

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Top half of the page, showing the overall synthesis pathways.
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The scheme shows the alkylation of acetoacetic ester. Mono- and di-alkylation at the C2 position is achieved using one equivalent of base and an alkyl halide. These alkylated esters can then be hydrolyzed and decarboxylated to form monosubstituted or disubstituted ketones. An alternative pathway using excess base forms a dianion, which is alkylated at the C4 position. This product can then be converted to a γ-diketone.
scheme_candidates.caption_text: R1 = 1°, 2° or 3° alkyl, aryl; R2 = 1° or 2° alkyl, allyl, benzyl; R3 = 1° or 2° alkyl, allyl, benzyl; base: NaH, NaOR1, LiHMDS, NaHMDS
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The preparation of ketones via the C-alkylation of esters of 3-oxobutanoic acid (acetoacetic esters) is called the acetoacetic ester synthesis. Acetoacetic esters can be deprotonated at either the C2 or at both the C2 and C4 carbons, depending on the amount of base used. The C-H bonds on the C2 carbon atom are activated by the electron-withdrawing effect of the two neighboring carbonyl groups. These protons are fairly acidic (pKa ~11 for C2 and pKa ~24 for C4), so the C2 position is deprotonated first in the presence of one equivalent of base (sodium alkoxide, LDA, NaHMDS or LiHMDS, etc.). The resulting anion can be trapped with various alkylating agents. A second alkylation at C2 is also possible with another equivalent of base and alkylating agent. When an acetoacetic ester is subjected to excess base, the corresponding dianion (extended enolate) is formed. When an electrophile (e.g., alkyl halide) is added to the dianion, alkylation occurs first at the most nucleophilic (reactive) C4 position. The resulting alkylated acetoacetic ester derivatives can be subjected to two types of hydrolytic cleavage, depending on the conditions: 1) dilute acid hydrolyzes the ester group, and the resulting β-keto acid undergoes decarboxylation to give a ketone (mono- or disubstituted acetone derivative); 2) aqueous base induces a retro-Claisen reaction to afford acids after protonation. The hydrolysis by dilute acid is most commonly used, since the reaction mixture is not contaminated with by-products derived from ketonic scission. More recently the use of the Krapcho decarboxylation allows neutral decarboxylation conditions. As with malonic ester, monoalkyl derivatives of acetoacetic ester undergo a base-catalyzed coupling reaction in the presence of iodine. Hydrolysis and decarboxylation of the coupled products produce γ-diketones. The starting acetoacetic esters are most often obtained via the Claisen condensation of the corresponding esters, but other methods are also available for their preparation.

extracts.transformation_text: C2 monoalkylation of acetoacetic ester
C2 dialkylation of acetoacetic ester
Hydrolysis and decarboxylation to monosubstituted ketone
Hydrolysis and decarboxylation to disubstituted ketone
C4 monoalkylation of acetoacetic ester
Formation of γ-Diketone

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Bottom half of the page, showing the detailed mechanism.
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The mechanism is depicted in three stages: Alkylation, Hydrolysis, and Decarboxylation. Alkylation proceeds via an enolate intermediate which attacks an alkyl halide in an SN2 reaction. The resulting ester is then hydrolyzed to a β-keto acid. Finally, the β-keto acid undergoes thermal decarboxylation through a six-membered transition state, forming an enol that tautomerizes to the final ketone product.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: Mechanism: The first step is the deprotonation of acetoacetic ester at the C2 position with one equivalent of base. The resulting enolate is nucleophilic and reacts with the electrophilic alkyl halide in an SN2 reaction to afford the C2 substituted acetoacetic ester, which can be isolated. The ester is hydrolyzed by treatment with aqueous acid to the corresponding β-keto acid, which is thermally unstable and undergoes decarboxylation via a six-membered transition state.

extracts.transformation_text: Alkylation
extracts.notes_text: SN2

extracts.transformation_text: Hydrolysis
Decarboxylation
extracts.notes_text: Reaction proceeds via a six-membered transition state.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A multi-step synthesis of a bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane skeleton, a substructure of Solanoeclepin A, starting from tert-butyl acetoacetate. The key steps include an acetoacetic ester synthesis, formation of a dioxenone, an intramolecular photochemical [2+2] cycloaddition, and a reduction.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: In the laboratory of H. Hiemstra, the synthesis of the bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane substructure of solanoeclepin A was undertaken utilizing the intramolecular photochemical dioxenone-alkene [2+2] cycloaddition reaction. The dioxenone precursor was prepared from the commercially available tert-butyl acetoacetate using the acetoacetic ester synthesis. When this dioxenone precursor was subjected to irradiation at 300 nm, complete conversion of the starting material was observed after about 4h, and the expected cycloadduct was formed in acceptable yield.

extracts.transformation_text: Alkylation of tert-butyl acetoacetate with allyl bromide.
Formation of a dioxenone precursor from an alkylated acetoacetate derivative.
extracts.notes_text: Yield is for this step and the previous one combined.

extracts.transformation_text: Intramolecular photochemical cycloaddition of a dioxenone-alkene.
Reduction of a lactone to a diol.
extracts.notes_text: Yield is for this step and the previous one combined.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A two-step synthesis of a 2-hydroxy-3-acetylfuran derivative via the Feist-Bénary reaction. The process involves the alkylation of t-butylacetoacetate with an α-bromoketone, followed by acid-catalyzed cyclization.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: R. Neier et al. synthesized substituted 2-hydroxy-3-acetylfurans by the alkylation of tert-butylacetoacetate with an α-haloketone, followed by treatment of the intermediate with trifluoroacetic acid. When furans are prepared from β-ketoesters and α-haloketones, the reaction is known as the Feist-Bénary reaction. A second alkylation of the C2 alkylated intermediate with various bromoalkanes yielded 2,2-disubstituted products, which upon treatment with TFA, provided access to trisubstituted furans.

extracts.transformation_text: Alkylation of t-butylacetoacetate with an α-bromoketone.
extracts.conditions_text: 1. NaH (1.1 equiv), THF, 30 min, 0 °C then 2-bromo-1-phenylpropan-1-one (1.1 equiv), 0 °C, 2h then r.t., 12h

extracts.transformation_text: Acid-catalyzed cyclization to form a furan derivative.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A synthesis of tetrahydrofuran and tetrahydropyran derivatives by reacting a dianion of an acetoacetic ester with an epibromohydrin derivative. The reaction shows high selectivity for the tetrahydrofuran product when LiClO4 is used as an additive.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: M. Nakada and co-workers developed a novel synthesis of tetrahydrofuran and tetrahydropyran derivatives by reacting dianions of acetoacetic esters with epibromohydrin derivatives. The selective formation of the tetrahydrofuran derivatives was achieved by the use of LiClO4 as an additive.

extracts.transformation_text: Reaction of an acetoacetic ester dianion with a bromohydrin derivative to form furan and pyran rings.
extracts.notes_text: The ratio of Tetrahydrofuran to Tetrahydropyran product is 135:1.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A synthetic strategy towards the 1-azabicyclo[5.3.0]decane ring system, the core of Stemona alkaloids. The synthesis involves a successive double alkylation of the dianion of ethyl acetoacetate.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: A synthetic strategy was developed for the typical core structure of the Stemona alkaloids in the laboratory of C.H. Heathcock. The precursor for the 1-azabicyclo[5.3.0]decane ring system was prepared via the successive double alkylation of the dianion of ethyl acetoacetate.

extracts.transformation_text: First alkylation of the dianion of ethyl acetoacetate with an iodo-ketal.
Multi-step sequence involving a second alkylation and cyclization to form a bicyclic precursor.
extracts.conditions_text: 1. LiI, DME, heat; 2. NaOMe, MeOH; 3. set pH 1; 4. toluene, heat
extracts.notes_text: The product is a precursor for the 1-azabicyclo[5.3.0]decane system.
Target core structure of Stemona alkaloids.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A general scheme for the acyloin condensation shows two ester molecules reacting with molten sodium metal and TMSCl in refluxing xylene to form a bis-silyloxyalkene. Subsequent acidic workup with H+/H2O yields the final acyloin product.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The acyloin condensation affords acyloins (α-hydroxy ketones) by treating aliphatic esters with molten, highly dispersed sodium in hot xylene. The resulting disodium acyloin derivatives are acidified to liberate the corresponding acyloins, which are valuable synthetic intermediates. Aliphatic monoesters give symmetrical compounds, while diesters lead to cyclic acyloins. The intramolecular acyloin condensation is one of the best ways of closing rings of 10 members or more (up to 34 membered rings were synthesized). For the preparation of aromatic acyloins (R=Ar), the benzoin condensation between two aromatic aldehydes is applied. The acyloin condensation is performed in an inert atmosphere, since the acyloins and their anions are readily oxidized. For small rings (ring size: 4-6), yields are greatly improved in the presence of TMSCl and by the use of ultrasound. The addition of TMSCl increases the scope of this reaction by preventing base-catalyzed side reactions such as β-elimination, Claisen or Dieckmann condensations. The resulting bis-silyloxyalkenes are either isolated or converted into acyloins by simple hydrolysis or alcoholysis.

extracts.transformation_text: Ester dimerization to acyloin
extracts.reagents_text: molten sodium metal, TMSCl, H+ / H2O

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: Mechanism A shows the conversion of two ester molecules to an acyloin. The pathway involves reduction to a radical anion, dimerization, elimination of alkoxide to a diketone, further reduction, acidic work-up to an enediol, and finally tautomerization to the acyloin, which is highlighted in a box.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: There are currently two proposed mechanisms for the acyloin ester condensation. In mechanism A the sodium reacts with the ester in a single electron transfer (SET) process to give a radical anion species, which can dimerize to a dialkoxy dianion. Elimination of two alkoxide anions gives a diketone. Further reduction (electron transfer from the sodium metal to the diketone) leads to a new dianion, which upon acidic work-up yields an enediol that tautomerizes to an acyloin.

extracts.transformation_text: Mechanism A for Acyloin Condensation
extracts.intermediates_text: radical anion, dialkoxy dianion, diketone, dianion, enediol
extracts.notes_text: The mechanism involves reduction, dimerization, elimination, further reduction, acidic work-up, and tautomerization.

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: Mechanism B shows an alternative pathway from an ester to an acyloin via an epoxide intermediate. The pathway proceeds to a diketone, which is then reduced, hydrolyzed to an enediol, and tautomerizes to the final acyloin product, highlighted in a box.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: In mechanism B an epoxide intermediate is proposed.

extracts.transformation_text: Mechanism B for Acyloin Condensation
extracts.intermediates_text: radical anion, epoxide intermediate, diketone, dianion, enediol
extracts.notes_text: The mechanism involves reduction, intramolecular attack, elimination, rearrangement through an epoxide intermediate, further reduction, acidic work-up, and tautomerization.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Four examples of ultrasound-promoted cyclizations from the work of J. Salaün.
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: Four related cyclization reactions are shown. The first two are acyloin condensations of a 1,4-diester and a 1,5-diester to form four- and five-membered rings, respectively. The second two are reductive cyclizations of a chloroester and a bromoester to form three-membered rings. All reactions use Na/TMSCl and sonochemical activation.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: J. Salaün and co-workers studied the ultrasound-promoted acyloin condensation and cyclization of carboxylic esters. They found that the acyloin coupling of 1,4-, 1,5-, and 1,6-diesters afforded 4-, 5- and 6-membered ring products. The cyclization of β-chloroesters to 3-membered ring products in the presence of TMSCl, which previously required highly dispersed sodium, was simplified and improved under sonochemical activation.

extracts.transformation_text: Intramolecular acyloin condensation of a 1,4-diester to a 1,2-bis(silyloxy)cyclobutene.
Intramolecular acyloin condensation of a 1,5-diester to a 1,2-bis(silyloxy)cyclopentene.
Reductive cyclization of an alpha-chloro ester to a silylated cyclopropanol derivative.
Reductive cyclization of a beta-bromo ester to a silylated cyclopropanol derivative.

scheme_candidates.bbox_note: Synthesis of a precursor to Anopterine.
scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A two-step synthesis is shown starting from a complex diester. The first step is an intramolecular acyloin condensation using Na/TMSCl in refluxing PhMe to give a bis-silyloxyalkene in 90% yield. The second step is a deprotection using HF in MeCN to yield a diol, which is a precursor for future work towards Anopterine.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: The diterpene alkaloids of the Anopterus species, of which anopterine (R=tigloyl) is a major constituent, are associated with a high level of antitumor activity. All of these alkaloids contain the tricyclo[3.3.2.1]decane substructure. S. Sieburth et al. utilized the acyloin condensation as a key step in the short construction of this tricyclic framework.

extracts.transformation_text: Intramolecular acyloin condensation of a complex diester.
Deprotection of silyl ethers to form a diol.
extracts.notes_text: Future work

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: The chemical structure of Anopterine is shown in a box, with an R group defined as tigloyl.

extracts.notes_text: R = tigloyl

scheme_candidates.vision_summary: A two-step synthesis of a bicyclic diketone is shown. First, a diester tethered to an acetal undergoes an acyloin condensation with Na/TMSCl in refluxing toluene to yield a bis-silyloxyalkene (82% yield). This intermediate is then treated sequentially with BF3*OEt2 in DCM at -78 C, warmed to room temperature, and then treated with TFA to afford the final bicyclic diketone. The overall yield for the two steps is 36%.
scheme_candidates.nearby_text: D.J. Burnell et al. synthesized bicyclic diketones by Lewis acid-promoted geminal acylation involving cyclic acyloins tethered to an acetal. The required bis-silyloxyalkenes were prepared by using the standard acyloin condensation conditions.

extracts.transformation_text: Acyloin condensation of a diester to a bis-silyloxyalkene.
Lewis acid-promoted geminal acylation to form a bicyclic diketone.
extracts.reagents_text:
1. BF3*OEt2 (2 equiv)
2. warm-up to r.t.
then
TFA (10 equiv)
